questions.question: According to the Mental Health Recovery Model (MHRM), how does 'personal recovery' differ from 'clinical recovery'?
options: There is no difference — recovery always means complete symptom elimination
Personal recovery focuses on hope, meaning, identity, and quality of life beyond symptom reduction; clinical recovery focuses on symptom elimination
Personal recovery is less important than clinical recovery
Personal recovery is only for people who refuse medication or therapy
questions.explanation: The Mental Health Recovery Model distinguishes CLINICAL RECOVERY (symptom reduction/elimination) from PERSONAL RECOVERY (hope, meaning, identity, quality of life, community participation, satisfying life even with ongoing symptoms or limitations). Personal recovery emphasizes that wellbeing is more than absence of symptoms — it includes meaningful activities, identity beyond illness, and life satisfaction. Recreational participation is CENTRAL to personal recovery.

questions.question: What distinguishes licensed recreational therapy (RT) from informal recreational activities?
options: Licensed RT is just organized fun activities led by anyone in a recreational setting

Licensed RT is delivered by CTRS (Certified Therapeutic Recreation Specialists), involves systematic assessment, individualized treatment planning, functional goals, and evidence-based clinical intervention
Licensed RT is only for children and physical disabilities, not mental health
There is no real difference — any enjoyable activity counts as recreational therapy
questions.explanation: Licensed recreational therapy is a clinical allied health profession delivered by CTRS (Certified Therapeutic Recreation Specialists with bachelor's/master's degrees and national certification). It involves: systematic functional assessment, individualized treatment plans, measurable goals, evidence-informed interventions, and clinical oversight. It is NOT simply 'fun activities' — it's systematic clinical intervention for functional impairments. Informal recreation is self-directed wellness activity without clinical assessment or oversight.

questions.question: In which situations is licensed recreational therapy (RT) most appropriate? (Select all that apply)
options: Significant functional impairment preventing recreational participation
Severe social isolation with inability to access community activities
Mild stress from work that could benefit from occasional walks
Severe anhedonia (inability to experience pleasure) requiring systematic intervention
Cognitive or physical limitations requiring adaptive recreation approaches
Someone who just wants to join a recreational sports league for fun
questions.explanation: Licensed RT is appropriate when: (1) Significant functional impairment exists (complete loss of recreational participation); (2) Severe social isolation prevents community access; (3) Severe anhedonia requires systematic leisure education and behavioral activation; (4) Cognitive/physical limitations need adaptive approaches. Mild stress or wanting to join sports leagues for fun are appropriate for informal self-directed recreation, not clinical RT.

questions.question: What is the appropriate relationship between recreational therapy (or informal recreation) and evidence-based psychiatric treatment (medication, psychotherapy)?
options: Recreation should replace medication and therapy because it's more natural and holistic
Recreation is adjunctive and complementary — it supports recovery alongside (not instead of) evidence-based treatment
Recreation is only appropriate after you've fully recovered from mental illness
Recreation and psychiatric treatment are unrelated and shouldn't be discussed together
questions.explanation: Recreation (whether licensed RT or informal) is ADJUNCTIVE to evidence-based psychiatric treatment — it complements medication and therapy, addressing quality of life, identity, functional capacity, and community participation that treatment alone doesn't fully restore. Recreation does NOT replace clinical treatment. For people with moderate-to-severe mental illness, continuing medication and therapy while engaging in recreational recovery practices is the evidence-supported approach.

questions.question: Someone in depression recovery wants to re-engage with hobbies but has very low energy and anhedonia (can't feel pleasure). What is the MOST recovery-supportive approach?
options: Wait until motivation and energy return before attempting any recreational activities
Force yourself to immediately resume pre-illness recreational intensity to 'push through'

Start where you are NOW (micro-dose recreation at current capacity like 10-15 min), use behavioral activation (schedule activities even without motivation), track mood improvements post-activity, celebrate small wins
Give up on recreation entirely since you can't enjoy it anyway
questions.explanation: The most recovery-supportive approach uses BEHAVIORAL ACTIVATION principles: (1) Start at current capacity (even 10-15 min if that's what's realistic); (2) Schedule activities in advance (don't wait for motivation); (3) Engage even when you don't 'feel like it' (mood often improves post-activity); (4) Track activity and mood (notice improvements); (5) Celebrate small wins (showing up is success). Waiting for motivation or forcing pre-illness intensity both fail. Anhedonia is addressed through graduated engagement, not avoidance.
